text: laparoscopic robotic-assisted conversion of duodenal switch to partial gastrectomy with a Roux-en-Y gastrodegynostomy. A fifty-four-year-old woman with a history of cholecystectomy, open duodenal switch, severe gastroesophageal reflux disease, hiatal hernia, obesity body mass index forty-four point seven, underwent a biliopancreatic diversion with a duodenal degenostomy many years ago in an open fashion. She later developed weight regain and severe gastroesophageal reflux with great deesophagitis and a demister score of seventy two point four. An upper GI showed that the stomach was very dilated with a stricture at the duodenal jejunostomy.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. Trocar placement is visible, and the da Vinci robotic system is set up for the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing multiple adhesions between the small bowel, abdominal wall, omentum, and colon. After fifty minutes, the scar tissue is meticulously dissected and removed, freeing the abdomen from adhesions. A Nathanson liver retractor is positioned at the epigastrium.
The surgeon continues dissection of the small bowel, freeing it from the colon and maneuvering the retrocholic alimentary limb from the inframesocolic area to the supracolic area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the dilated stomach, separating the omentum from the suture line of the sleeve gastrectomy. The mesenterium is divided using a vessel sealer, and the small bowel is divided with an endogea tan load.
The surgeon detaches the posterior attachments of the left gastrectomy, identifying and preserving the left gastric artery while taking down the right gastric artery.

The surgeon creates a five-centimeter gastric pouch using an Endogea black load 60 stapler. A 40 French Blanc bougie is inserted into the gastric pouch to guide its formation. The remnant distal stomach and duodenal jejunostomy are placed into an endopouch and extracted through a 15-millimeter trocar.

The surgeon dissects the hiatus, opens the gastroesophageal membrane, and accesses the mediastinum. A retroesophageal window is created, allowing the esophagus to be completely dissected into the mediastinum and mobilized back to the abdomen with three centimeters of esophagus without tension.
The surgeon repairs the hiatal hernia using two interrupted 0-Ethibond sutures, securing the anatomical structures at the hiatus.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to bring the alimentary limb into the upper abdomen, creating a new gastrogenostomy. The anastomosis is constructed in two layers: the posterior layer with a running three-zero PDS V-lock suture, and the inner and external layers with running three-zero PDS sutures.
The surgeon clamps the alimentary limb and performs an intraoperative endoscopy, revealing a well-constructed anastomosis, a small gastric pouch, and no signs of leakage.

The surgeon reattaches the omentum to the lateral suture line of the gastric pouch using 3-0 PDS V-lock sutures to release tension and stabilize the pouch. The surgical field shows no intraoperative blood loss, and no drains are visible.
The patient was discharged on the second day after surgery tolerating a blenderized diet. No complications were recorded.
